2. Bayesian Hypothesis Testing

2.1. Prelude

Hypothesis testing is a natural framework for decision-making from available data, .

Frequentist vs Bayesian Hypothesis Testing1

  • Probability:

    • F: parameters are fixed but unknown. Probability are long-run frequencies over hypothetical reptitions of the experiment
    • B: Both data and parameters are treated as RVs. Observation is realization of RV.

  • What is tested

    • F: How incompatible data is with null hypothesis
    • B: Compare hypotheses directly using Bayes rule.

  • Decision criteria

    • F: Control error rates under repeated sampling
    • B: Minimize expected loss under the posterior

Frequentist is a subset of nonbayesian, which includes minimax for example.

Abstraction:

  1. Model phenomenon
  2. Model observation mechanism
  3. Design processors
  4. Analyze performance (e.g. precision and recall for hypothesis tests, bias and covariance for estimators)

2.2. Model

Setup: is a random variable, The true hypothesis on the population parameter is a categorical random variable (assumption). We want to classify given .

Definition 2.1 (Generative assumption in Bayesian Hypothesis Testing). In Bayesian hypothesis testing, we assume a complete description of our model is described by the a priori probabilities

(2.1)

together with a characterization of the observed data under each hypothesis, which takes the form of the conditional probablity distributions:

(2.2)

Remark. This formulation not compatible with composite hypotheses because you can’t define if is composite. You gotta define a prior on within each hypothesis. [TODO] This is called hierarchical Bayesian model.

Bayes set the goal to be minimizing the expected loss (Bayes risk2):

Remarks

  • is a precomputable threshold etermined from the a priori probabilities and costs.
  • is an example of a sufficient statistic, i.e. knowledge of is as good as knowledge of itself.
  • for any strictly increasing function also is a sufficient statistic. For example, natural log.
  • A neural network usually does not compute since we don’t know the true . When is not computable exactly, Theorem 2.1 doesn’t hold and it makes sense for NNs to compute multiple features.
  • For hypotheses, we use features, each likelihood ratio for with respect to . It becomes an argmin over losses.
  • Even if our prior distributions (and the induced ) sucks, we can interpret the test as telling us which model better approximates the data (yields lower cost).

[COOL] The following example shows we can recover familiar decision rules using 0-1 loss.

Example 2.1 (0-1 loss). Optimal procesor for 0-1 loss minimizes error probability i.e. maximizing accuracy. This is also called the maximum a posteriori decision rule

(2.11)

When the hypotheses are equally likely, the error becomes maximum likelihood decision rule

(2.12)

2.4. [COOL] Remark on Forward Models

Recall we describe the a point as being generated after picking a label (Definition 2.1). The notes consider computing as a forward/discriminative problem that computes a hypothesis given a label.

Proposition 2.1. Any binary generative model (Definition 2.1) described by

(2.13)

implies a logistic posterior in a 1D statistic.

It turns out that statistic must be an affine transformation of the log likelihood ratio. I.e. we can run logistic regression on the LLR.

(2.14)

This seems like just fancy rearrangment, but it explains why the logistic regression/sigmoid function is canonical from a Bayesian viewpoint. We train NN classifiers to estimate this affine transformed LLR. For example,

(2.15)

The exact same algebra extends to explaining why softmax is canonical for multiclass generative models:
